tldr: Post-training Qwen2.5-32B/72B and ByT5-Base on Gemini-generated transliteration/translation pairs from published texts and PDFs, bypassing the official competition dataset.
summary: The team bypassed the official competition training data entirely, instead generating ~110,000 bidirectional translation pairs by feeding published texts and PDF pages into Gemini. They post-trained Qwen2.5-32B and Qwen2.5-72B using LoRA (rank 64, alpha 128) for 3 epochs on 8x B200s, alongside a 15-epoch ByT5-Base run at 3e-4 LR. Validation relied on a perturbed train.csv filtered for length consistency, yielding a CV of ~43, though the authors noted residual data leakage. Heavy regex-based preprocessing and postprocessing handled Akkadian-specific formatting, fractions, and gaps, while an LLM prompt was used for repetition removal.
extensive_summary:
## Solution Overview
The team competed under severe time constraints, leveraging an autonomous agent (`Qgentic-AI`) to iterate experiments. They explicitly rejected the competition's official training data, deeming it confusing and unhelpful. Instead, they constructed a custom dataset by prompting Gemini to split published texts into sentences and extract transliteration/translation pairs from PDF pages. After manual cleaning and LLM-as-a-judge filtering, they amassed ~55,000 pairs, expanding to ~110,000 for bidirectional (ENG↔AKK) training.

## Models & Training
They post-trained Qwen2.5-32B and Qwen2.5-72B for 3 epochs on 8x B200 GPUs using LoRA (rank 64, alpha 128). ByT5-Base was trained for 15 epochs at a learning rate of 3e-4. Hyperparameters were optimized by the Qgentic-AI agent.

## Preprocessing & Postprocessing
Preprocessing followed the host's discussion, focusing on Akkadian-specific normalization: converting Roman months to numerals, mapping decimals and ASCII fractions to Unicode fractions, normalizing subscript digits, and standardizing gap/break markers (`<gap>`). Specific domain mappings were applied (e.g., `PN` → `<gap>`, `-gold` → `pašallum gold`). Postprocessing removed repetitive phrases using a dedicated prompt on the base Qwen2.5-72B model, followed by regex cleanup to align gaps between transliteration and translation outputs.

## Validation & Critical Findings
Validation used a perturbed `train.csv` that excluded samples with significant length mismatches between train and validation sets, achieving a CV of ~43. The authors explicitly noted that data leakage persisted in this CV, so it should not be treated as fully trustworthy. 

## What Worked vs. What Did Not Work
**Worked:** Generating external data via Gemini OCR/pairs, post-training Qwen2.5 with LoRA, using ByT5-Base, and heavy regex/LLM-based text cleaning.
**Did Not Work:** ByT5 models ≥ Large, MBR (Minimum Bayes Risk), using the competition's official training data, Arabic intermediate translation, and training a separate judge model for translation quality.
cv_strategy: Perturbed train.csv filtered for train/validation length consistency; CV score ~43.
preprocessing: Regex-based normalization of Roman months, decimals, and ASCII fractions to Unicode fractions
Subscript digit conversion to standard numerals
Domain-specific term mappings (e.g., PN to <gap>, suffix replacements for gold/taxiles)
LLM prompt for repetition removal during postprocessing
post_processing: Removed repetitions using a base Qwen2.5-72B prompt, followed by regex cleanup to align gaps between transliteration and translation outputs and strip empty strings.
what_worked: Generating external translation pairs via Gemini OCR/published texts instead of using competition train data
Heavy regex preprocessing and LLM-based postprocessing for Akkadian formatting
what_did_not_work: Using the competition's official training data
Training a separate judge model to grade translations
critical_findings: Validation set contained residual data leakage despite length-mismatch filtering, making CV scores less trustworthy than expected.
The official competition training data was confusing and ultimately unhelpful, leading to its complete abandonment.
